data.title: HPI: This 9.7% Yielding Preferred Fund Is A Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

With market indices pulling back from their highs, it can be difficult to determine where the good opportunities are. However, the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund (HPI) now trades at an attractive valuation, so I wanted to reassess its overall value proposition. When I previously covered HPI, I issued a hold rating due to the limited growth potential related to leverage. Since then, the fund's share price has declined by more than 12.4%, and the total return has not kept up with the S&P 500 Index (SPX). However, the fund has released an updated report, and the interest rate environment has shifted. Therefore, I anticipate that performance will improve once we get over the current macroeconomic uncertainties.

Furthermore, the fund now trades at one of the most attractive ends of its historical range. For instance, HPI now trades at a very small premium to NAV of 0.26%. For reference, HPI has traded at an average premium to NAV of 2.47% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that the fund now trades at a more reasonable price-to-NAV range that may justify the accumulation of shares.

The fund now offers a dividend yield of 9.7%, and the fund continues to demonstrate its ability to generate earnings that exceed distributions. Therefore, HPI remains a great option for investors that want to prioritize a steady stream of income. Forward-looking, I believe that will substantially improve when interest rates trend lower and as the financial sector is capable of participating in the expansion of AI. So let's start by reviewing the underlying strategy that HPI implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, HPI now has total managed assets of $686.8M that are spread across 167 different holdings. What makes HPI unique is the focus on preferred stocks and preferred convertible securities, which helps put an emphasis on sustainable income generation. The fund also has an expense ratio of 1.2% of assets, which is high but a direct result of the active management within its portfolio. The turnover rate is typically around 35%, which means that management is constantly rotating into new positions to maintain healthy cash flows. According to the fact sheet, the fund's breakdown by asset class is as follows:

  • Preferred Securities: 56.25%
  • Corporate Bonds: 40.63%
  • U.S. Agency: 1.78%

Furthermore, the fund puts an emphasis on financial securities, and management specifically looks for securities that have strong cash flows and a high issuance of preferred shares. For instance, exposure to financials makes up 37.67% of assets. However, it's important to note that HPI maintains this exposure through income-focused securities and not through common equity. Therefore, the fund isn't structurally capable of fully participating in the upside growth of its own holdings.

When looking at the top holdings of the fund, we can see that the fund maintains exposure to many high-quality positions. For instance, Bank of America (BAC) is the largest holding at 6.05% of assets. This is followed by other notable positions like Citigroup (C), Morgan Stanley (MS), CMS Energy Corp. (CMS), Wells Fargo (WFC), and Citizens Financial Group (CFG), just to name a few. HPI also allocates most of its positions to securities with strong credit ratings. For reference, the breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 59.94%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 37.51%
  • Note Rated: 0.31%

Lastly, the fund tends to focus on long-dated maturities within its portfolio. For instance, 50.97% of assets are allocated to securities with a maturity above 30 years. However, many preferred stocks issued by banks don't always have a maturity date, so they are allocated to this 30-year category. The tradeoff is that longer-dated positions mean HPI will have a secure source of cash flow for many years to come. However, the longer-dated positions also mean that these positions are sensitive to a high-interest-rate environment. A high-interest rate directly causes the value of these positions to decline, which is why HPI's growth potential may be limited as long as interest rates are elevated.

Attractive Outlook

The biggest challenge for HPI is to thrive in a higher interest rate environment. Despite several interest rate cuts through 2025, the federal funds rate remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. Even during the Fed's latest meeting, it was decided that rates would be left unchanged. As we can see below, the fund has struggled to regain enough positive momentum to get back to its pre-pandemic levels. I anticipate that lower interest rates will continue to allow upside growth in its share price as the underlying operating spread improves.

For instance, the fund uses leverage in an effort to expand its earnings when possible. For instance, total effective leverage amounts to 38.64% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$257.1M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage while interest rates are elevated because it directly increases the risk profile of the fund. For instance, a high-interest rate will negatively impact the spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from securities. When interest rates eventually trend lower, the spread becomes wider and leads to higher earnings.

When reviewing the latest semi-annual report for 2026, we get some more detailed insights into the fund's underlying earnings structure. For instance, net investment income amounted to $0.56 per share, which is on track to land within its usual range according to prior years. This is an indication that the portfolio strategy has a very consistent level of income generation through different market cycles. However, you may notice that net investment income was much higher during the 2021 and 2022 periods, likely due to the healthier operating spreads when interest rates were lower.

Furthermore, a lower interest rate environment can increase the odds of large net realized gains being captured. For instance, HPI was able to capture net realized gains of $2.47 per share back in 2021 when interest rates were at near-zero levels. Assuming a similar performance through the rest of the 2026 period, HPI is on track to deliver positive net realized gains. However, the dependence on interest rates has impacted the rate of NAV growth over the last few years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $20.81 per share
  • 2022: $17.99 per share
  • 2023: $15.31 per share
  • 2024: $16.13 per share
  • 2025: $16.02 per share
  • 2026 (semi-annual): $16.10 per share

While the growth is traditionally linked to interest rates, I believe that HPI is becoming increasingly aligned to participate in the growth of AI. For instance, HPI still maintains exposure to utilities companies, such as Energy Transfer (ET), NextEra Energy (NEE), and Entergy Corp. (ETR). These companies essentially provide exposure to the picks and shovels of the AI infrastructure market and are directly aligned to benefit from the large capex spending of the hyperscalers.

Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI data center market can grow to $810.6B by 2033. This represents a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 23.9% over the next few years. Despite the exposure through preferred shares, the fund can still see some upside growth if it holds convertible securities but still provides a better risk-adjusted approach to the exposure. For instance, here are some of the positive metrics that HPI's holdings are already experiencing:

  • Entergy Corp: management estimates an 8% annual EPS growth with a $43B capital plan as data center demand surges.
  • NextEra Energy: expects up to 30GW of power for data centers by 2035.

Risks

Although I am optimistic about the outlook of the fund, there are some headwinds that investors should be aware of. The biggest risk is if interest rate cuts are delayed or they trend higher over the next twelve months. Since the majority of the portfolio is allocated to securities with 30-year maturities, an uptick in rates will cause values within HPI to decline. In this scenario, leverage will continue to make it difficult for earnings to improve going forward.

Furthermore, I want to emphasize that HPI only makes sense for investors that want to specifically focus on dividends over time. HPI isn't structurally built to fully participate in the upside growth of its holdings, so long-term performance may not be as attractive as a standard index-tracking ETF, such as the State Street Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF). A comparison of the two funds makes it very clear that a high dividend yield doesn't automatically translate to higher returns.

For instance, HPI's ten-year total return sits at 54.6%, which severely underperforms against XLF's total return of 216.8% over the same time frame. As we can see, HPI's share price has also eroded over the last ten years due to the impacts of leverage, higher rates, and occasionally paying out more than it earns. So if you want to prioritize high returns, a traditional approach ETF may be more suitable.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1235 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 9.7%. If we multiply this monthly payout by twelve months, we get an estimated annual distribution of $1.482 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report, we can see that the fund has historically generated more income than it distributed to shareholders. Furthermore, the fund has displayed that it is capable of generating earnings that can support several years' worth of payouts in the past. For instance, his was demonstrated back when interest rates were low in 2021:

2021's earnings of $3.86 per share / $1.482 annual payout = 2.6 years of coverage.

So when interest rates trend lower and the fund is in a better environment to thrive, I anticipate that HPI's coverage will become much stronger. The payout history does reveal that payouts were lowered at the end of 2019 and haven't recovered yet. However, I believe that management is making the right choice by not raising the dividends yet because it allows the NAV to remain stable over time. Despite the lack of dividend raises, the consistent dividend levels over the last decade mean that long-term holders are capable of creating their own dividend growth by reinvesting distributions or adding new capital over time.

To demonstrate this concept, I ran a backtest on an initial $100,000 investment at the start of 2017. This backtest assumes that no additional capital was invested after the initial $100k. However, it does assume that all dividends received were reinvested back into the accumulation of shares. By simply remaining invested and staying patient, investors would have seen their annual dividend income increase over the last few years.

2017 Dividend Income: $8,562

2025 Dividend Income: $14,546

Lastly, investors need to consider the fact that distributions received from HPI may not offer any tax efficiency for investors. For instance, the latest available Section 19(a) notice indicates that 77% of the distributions paid YTD were classified as net investment income. This means that most of the distributions would be classified as ordinary income, which has the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in HPI would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to limit their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that HPI is now an attractive buy based on its valuation and attractive outlook over the next twelve months. In a scenario where interest rates continue to trend downward, this will improve the fund's operating spread and encourage higher valuations across its portfolio. Furthermore, the portfolio is aligned to capture some of the growth of AI infrastructure through its utilities exposure. The reporting indicates that the portfolio is certainly capable of generating earnings that far exceed the distributions that are being paid. Lastly, the fund remains a great option for investors seeking a reliable source of income generation over time.

data.title: John Hancock 529 Static Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

• U.S. stocks extended a historically fast rebound that began in early April, although upward momentum slowed in the final quarter of the year.

• The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on AI, and the Fed's interest rate reductions of 0.25% in October and December.

• Healthcare, a laggard for much of 2025, was the top-performing sector in the S&P 500 Index during Q4.

Market review and outlook

U.S. stocks gained 2.66% in the fourth quarter, according to the S&P 500 Index, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence, and the U.S. Federal Reserve's (Fed's) interest rate reductions of 0.25% in October and December. Amid this favorable backdrop for higher-risk assets, the index closed the year just shy of its all-time high. Value stocks modestly outpaced growth in Q4, while large caps had only a slight advantage over small caps, as investors' appetite for risk waned versus the prior three months.

In the fourth quarter, the index's narrow advance was driven by the defensive-oriented healthcare sector (+12%), which particularly shined in November. The growth-oriented communication services sector rose about 7%, benefiting from the transformative potential of generative AI. All other groups lagged the index. Notably, IT, which represented 35% of the S&P 500 Index in Q4, rose about 1%.

International developed-market stocks advanced 4.89%, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index. The discrepancy between value and growth stocks was more pronounced in this index, again in favor of value over growth. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index notched a 4.72% rise. South Korea (+27%) and Taiwan (+10%) were two strong components with sizable representation in this index, bolstered by the strength in technology stocks in those markets. On the other hand, China finished the quarter with a roughly -7% return, giving back some of its gains since the April bottom.

U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds gained 1.1% in the fourth quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Quarterly performance was fueled by a combination of elevated starting yields and price appreciation, as bond prices rose amid falling interest rates and investor demand, supported by stable fundamentals.

The U.S. equity bull market is entering its fourth year, having put up double-digit gains in each of the past three, leaving investors wondering whether it can keep the streak alive. While multiyear stretches of positive equity returns are common, double-digit returns four years in row are few and far between. The naysayers point to elevated valuations, narrowness of the market, uncertainty around Fed policy amid upcoming leadership change, a softening labor market, and U.S. mid-term election risk. Yet, history has shown that bull markets don't die of old age alone, and while valuations are elevated, they're below prior cycle extremes. Bulls can also point to still-robust earnings expectations, a broadening of AI beneficiaries, fiscal support, increased capital spending, deregulation, rising mergers-and-acquisitions activity, and easing trade tensions. With the risks reasonably balanced and momentum still intact, the path of least resistance for the equity market may continue to be higher for now. The question is, can it reach double digits for the four-peat?

Contributors and detractors

The portfolio recorded a single-digit gain for the quarter and slightly trailed its blended benchmark, comprising a 70% weighting in the Russell 3000 Index and a 30% weighting in the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (net). Disciplined Value International Fund (John Hancock (MFC)) was a noteworthy detractor for the quarter. The fund's lower allocation to value stocks and higher allocation to growth versus the MSCI EAFE Value Index was a headwind in a quarter where international value equities strongly outperformed growth names. Disciplined Value Fund (John Hancock) also detracted. Stock selection in healthcare, communication services, industrials, and consumer discretionary proved disappointing for the quarter. Conversely, International Growth Fund (John Hancock) contributed on a relative basis, largely due to security selection in the financials sector. U.S. Growth Fund (John Hancock) also contributed, aided by stock selection in IT and industrials. A tactical overweight to international value equities further contributed, given the relatively strong performance of that group.

data.title: Franklin Rising Dividends Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

• Markets: US equities delivered solid gains over the fourth quarter of 2025, maintaining their upward trajectory against a backdrop of generally robust corporate earnings, despite mixed economic signals and investor concerns about stretched technology valuations. Continued monetary easing by the US Federal Reserve (Fed) helped investor sentiment. The outperformance of the Magnificent Seven mega-capitalization technology stocks during the quarter drove gains for the S&P 500 Index.

• Contributors: A lack of exposure to Meta Platforms (META) was the quarter's top relative individual contributor. Selection in the financials and industrials sectors contributed on a relative basis, as did a lack of exposure to real estate.

• Detractors: The key driver of underperformance was a lack of exposure to Google parent Alphabet (GOOGL) and several stocks in the information technology (IT) sector that advanced sharply but did not meet the portfolio's dividend screen for investment. Positioning in IT and materials weighed on returns, along with a lack of exposure to communication services.

• Outlook: We have made a number of changes to the portfolio, which we believe better positions it to perform across market cycles, increasing allocations to high-conviction areas and key secular themes. We believe greater exposure to such secular growth themes as artificial intelligence (AI), energy transition, digitalization, innovation in biopharma and growth in wealth management should help the portfolio. Looking ahead to 2026, we believe the equity market environment could continue to broaden and provide opportunities across a wide range of sectors. We see attractive opportunities for dividend growth investors as many companies with strong balance sheets, pricing power and disciplined capital allocation continue to differentiate themselves from more leveraged or speculative peers. Our focus remains on identifying high-quality companies with durable competitive advantages and the financial flexibility that can help them compound income and total return for shareholders through a full market cycle. As demonstrated by the new additions during the fourth quarter of 2025, we remain optimistic about the opportunities we see across sectors.

Performance Review

• Over the quarter, Franklin Rising Dividends Fund (Advisor Class without sales charges) underperformed its benchmark, the S&P 500 Index, due to stock selection, especially in the IT sector.

• A lack of exposure to Google parent Alphabet accounted for more than half of the portfolio's quarterly relative underperformance as the shares advanced sharply. Alphabet does not meet the portfolio's dividend screen for investment. Several stocks the portfolio did not hold in the IT sector also drove underperformance.

• Within the portfolio's holdings, the largest detractor was enterprise cloud software provider Oracle (ORCL). Rising investor concern over the scale and execution risk of Oracle's aggressive data-center expansion weighed on sentiment. Questions have emerged around Oracle's ability to convert large AI-related bookings into near-term revenue, including uncertainty over the pace and durability of spending from major customers, even as capital expenditures accelerated sharply. Quarterly results showed slowing momentum in remaining performance obligations, or future expected revenues from existing contracts, a key metric for software/infrastructure-as-a-service providers.

• A lack of exposure to Facebook parent Meta Platforms, which has often weighed on relative performance in recent quarters, was the quarter's top individual contributor. The stock underperformed amid investor concerns around increasing operating costs and capital expenditures, tied to substantial investments in AI-related infrastructure. Among portfolio holdings, pharmaceutical firm Eli Lilly and Co. (LLY), a significant overweight, was the top contributor. Its shares advanced sharply during the period as accelerating weight-loss drugs sales helped the company deliver better-than-expected quarterly revenue and earnings. Lilly raised its full-year guidance, citing sustained demand for obesity and diabetes drugs. Approvals from the US Food & Drug Administration, along with positive news on oral glucagon-like peptide-1 candidate orforglipron, reinforced optimism that Lilly can broaden access to obesity treatment and extend its category leadership.

Outlook

• We remain cautiously optimistic, despite ongoing tariff-related uncertainty. Our view is that the United States will likely reach trade agreements with key partners, which should support economic growth. We believe further support could come from favorable tax policy, deregulation, onshoring and infrastructure spending. Despite the positive backdrop, we anticipate that corporate earnings could be mixed, given that prior tariff fears have resulted in some uneven order patterns. We believe the portfolio is well-positioned, should near-term turbulence return in equity markets, given our focus on resilient companies that can drive free cash flow during tougher environments. We are focused on determining the earnings outlook for our portfolio holdings, as well as those of companies we are considering for investment.

• We actively monitor key gauges of economic activity, as well as policy announcements, to evaluate if adjustments to position sizes are warranted. One positive development is that inflation is trending toward the Fed's goal.

• We aim for attractive performance across full market cycles. As market volatility increased at times in 2025, we saw some opportunities to add to certain holdings. We believe the portfolio is well-positioned for a range of economic outcomes.

• We remain encouraged by dividend trends for our portfolio companies, which have continued to grow their dividends faster than the overall market. Despite elevated uncertainty and some recent reductions in earnings estimates for the broader market, earnings are still expected to grow for the S&P 500 Index this year, implying that dividend growth could follow. We regard recent company announcements in relation to dividend increases as encouraging. Even if trends were to soften, we believe dividend growth for companies held in the portfolio has the potential to outpace that of the broader market over time.

data.title: Oil Shock Meets Asset Price Deflation
data.description:
Canada’s economy has generated no economic growth in five months and no job growth in eight months. Meanwhile, the household wealth effect is in reverse, with home sale prices nationally down 20% over the past 4 years and the stock market negative year-to-date.

The S&P 500 and Canada’s TSX are both off more than 7% and back to their levels last July and December 2025, respectively. Financials are falling. The US small-cap index is off more than 9% and back where it was in November 2024.

Tech is making matters worse, with the Nasdaq off more than 12%. International exposure isn’t helping; the MSCI all-country stock index, which covers large and mid-cap stocks across 23 developed and 24 emerging markets, is -8% since February and back to where it was last August.

No shelter from the storm: defence and health care stocks are off double digits, too. Still, more mean reversion is due: thus far, equities and home prices remain at the high, unattractive end of historic valuation levels.

Against this backdrop, fears of persistent oil-price inflation have caused Treasury yields to rise as the futures market is pricing in two Bank of Canada rate hikes in 2026, with a 30% chance of a third.

This has driven up interest rates so that Canadian households are borrowing at an average rate of 4.8% or 3.0% in real terms (CPI at 1.8% in February) – 70 bps above the average for the past three decades.

Since the start of the war in Iran, gasoline has been up approximately 30%, and diesel has been up around 40% across Canada, according to the Canadian Fuels Association.

No one knows how long fossil fuels will remain elevated, but housing accounts for a larger share of Canada’s cost of living index - the largest single component at 30%. The race is on to see how long central banks can watch from the sidelines as the job and asset markets weaken. The discussion here is on point.

Disclosure: No positions

data.description:
Fund performance

▪ Columbia Short Term Bond Fund Institutional Class shares returned 1.16% for the three months ending December 31, 2025. The fund's benchmark Bloomberg 1-3 Year Government/Credit Index returned 1.16% for the same period.

▪ Most fixed-income sectors recorded positive excess returns over similar-duration Treasuries during the fourth quarter, with spread movements being mixed across sectors (duration measures the sensitivity of a bond to changes in interest rates). The fund's out-of-benchmark allocation to structured products was the largest contributor to relative outperformance. High-yield corporates also contributed positively to relative performance.

▪ The fund's duration was slightly long relative to the benchmark, with exposure to maturities longer than three years. The fund was underweight in one-to-three-year maturities to balance the overweight to longer maturities. The net impact was that duration and yield-curve positioning modestly detracted during the quarter. The yield curve steepened, with maturities shorter than three years seeing their yields rally, while longer maturities sold off. The two-year Treasury yield decreased eight basis points (bps), while the five-year Treasury yield increased five bps. (A basis point is 1/100th of one percent.)

Market overview

Returns were positive across the U.S. bond market for the fourth quarter. With indicators suggesting that economic growth was softening, the U.S. Federal Reserve followed up September's quarter-point reduction in its benchmark overnight lending rate with equivalent cuts in October and December, leaving the fed funds target rate in the 3.50% to 3.75% range. In conjunction with its October meeting, the Fed announced an end to the quantitative tightening program under which it had reduced the size of its balance sheet by not reinvesting the proceeds from maturing bonds. The Fed's December commentary cited a softening employment backdrop but also indicated caution with respect to additional rate cuts, given lingering inflation concerns. The Treasury yield curve steepened slightly over the quarter while the 10-year Treasury yield finished essentially flat, moving from 4.16% to 4.18%.

Quarterly portfolio update

Collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs), asset-backed securities (ABS) and commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) continued to represent the fund's largest out-of-benchmark allocations. During the quarter, the fund's exposure to CMOs and ABS decreased. Within CMBS, the fund continued to focus its exposure on high-quality hospitality, industrial and retail properties. All structured sectors had positive excess returns during the period. The underlying collateral performance for the fund's high-quality structured securities remained solid, with the U.S. consumer in relatively good financial health. Therefore, we maintain a constructive outlook for non-agency CMOs, ABS and CMBS, and believe their yield advantage over Treasuries offers relative value.

The fund's investment-grade corporate holdings generated positive total returns but did not generate excess returns during the quarter. Within the short-duration corporate market, subsectors produced mixed excess returns against similar duration Treasuries. The brokerage, consumer cyclicals and transportation subsectors produced the largest excess returns. Investment-grade corporate fundamentals remained stable during the quarter and are well positioned to withstand continued economic uncertainty.

The fund's high-yield corporate exposure decreased modestly throughout the quarter. High-yield spreads modestly tightened, and the sector continued to rally. High-yield corporates outperformed higher-quality investment-grade corporates on an excess-return basis.

Outlook

During the fourth quarter, the Fed cut the federal funds rate by 50 bps across two cuts. This marked three consecutive cuts to finish the year; however, the pace of future easing remains uncertain. Interest-rate volatility has continued the downward trend it has followed since the initial tariff pause in mid-April, and despite modest widening in some sectors, spreads remain near historically tight levels. As the consumer and corporate impact from the combination of policy changes, trade uncertainty and a historically long government shutdown continues to develop, we will continue to closely monitor economic activity. More specifically, we will focus on the labor market, consumer data, inflation, inflation expectations and the impact that changing interest rates are having on consumer and corporate balance sheets. Given changing interest rates and the economic backdrop, the fund is positioning duration to be slightly longer than its benchmark.

Going forward, the fund will likely maintain its U.S. Treasury underweight in favor of allocations to spread sectors. In general, we believe credit-related valuations are still somewhat attractive, and we continue to work closely with our credit research analysts to proactively identify attractive relative-value opportunities. Regarding sectors, we believe structured products, in particular non-agency CMOs, CMBS and ABS, offer relative value and are attractive at current spreads.

Investment-grade corporate allocations are now neutral relative to the benchmark. Within the corporate market, the fund is underweight the non-corporate 1 and the consumer-cyclicals sectors, and overweight the banking, consumer non-cyclicals and electric utilities sectors. Investment-grade supply for the quarter was $321.4 billion, which outpaced the fourth quarter of 2024's $237.7 billion. (Source: Barclays Live)

data.title: Is The War Really Reaching Its End? Assets Bounce Despite Oil Rally - Market Check
data.description:
By Elior Manier

We are now officially entering the fifth week of the US-Iran-Israel conflict, which sent bombs flying all over the Middle East, but more concerningly, sent global assets flying all over.

The main culprit was crude oil prices – rallying about 50% since its monthly open, the commodity hasn't failed to contribute its fair part in overall volatility.

After sustaining a broad, inverted correlation with most asset classes and currencies, this trend appears to be abating. Traders are now really looking to relax their preceding angst with the US entering into more consistent negotiations with their enemy-counterpart in the Islamic regime, and Israel also prepares for final waves of attack to dampen the military reconstruction efforts.

Black Gold is now at a spot where uncertainty is priced in, leaving only a premium for the proper lack of supply that would traditionally go through the infamous Strait of Hormuz. Brent has been stuck above $110 since the weekly open, and WTI remains well above $100.

Key levels to watch for WTI:

To the upside:

  • $106 - Closing above could maintain further bullish pressure
  • $110 - Psychological level not seen since the mid-March spike
  • $120 - War highs; above this, things could get catastrophic for the economy

To the downside:

  • $100 - Correcting back below would boost the current ease in sentiment significantly
  • $90 - Short-term momentum turns bearish for the commodity. Markets should pick up their rebound
  • $85 - Any move below this would confirm that the situation is indeed not worsening, the best sign for markets. Every asset becomes a buy on a daily close below.

Oil rising isn't such a surprise to most of us, but the more peculiar change in today's flows comes from the fact that despite this rise, bonds are rallying (yields are lower - implying lower inflation expectations), stocks bounced but seem to remain under pressure (at least, not worsening for now), and metals have formed a more consistent bottom.

If anything was dampening market mood throughout last week, it was the fact that failed diplomatic attempts could not generate a much larger continuum of tranquility.

But if the current, more realistic, conversations really turn into something positive, the 5-week period could be precise, and next week could be a great opportunity to join a bounce.

It is, of course, very early to say, considering that the US president and his administration are so unpredictable, and it would be a mistake to assume that the Iranian regime is not.

In any case, month-end is approaching and could bring significant changes to the flows. Participants have been accustomed to it throughout this long and crazy March.

US Treasuries are rallying, the most optimistic sign

US bonds (and fixed income in general) were among the worst performers across all asset classes this month, under intense pressure from rising inflation expectations.

With the tumble in fixed income (and rising yields), bond vigilantes were pricing out all types of rate cuts across the globe, implying that the repercussions of rising oil would prevent lower rates and even lead to some monetary policy rises (as seen in Europe and England).

Why is it important to check bonds in this environment? This asset class is the most reactive to risk news and inflation expectations. If they ease, other assets will be subject to much less constraint (as stocks tend to see delayed bounces in such an environment).

Despite their morning bounce, they still have a lot to recover, and their bounce is proving challenged by the fresh bid in oil.

  • For the 30Y yield, keep a close eye on 5.00% to the upside, 4.75% to the downside.
  • For the 2Y yield, 4.03% to the upside (+ hikes priced in) and 3.75% to the downside.

Metals rebound but still under pressure

Metals have indeed marked their bottom, but the rest will be to see if they can actually maintain a higher path for longer. Their nature as diversification assets holds them in the middle of two different narratives:

  • Are they going to rally from the lower hike pricings?
  • Are they going to resume their drops from the drop in uncertainty?

In this mix of fate, it seems that range-bound conditions may prevail in the precious commodities until a breakout follows.

The US dollar remains on top of its game

Despite the easing narrative, the US dollar remains a top dog in this weekly open. The Dollar Index is pointing towards a breakout, but failing here may also form a double top, so expect this monthly close to be a significant indicator for what's to come for FX markets in April.

The direction is for now difficult to predict, with the latest rally being very persistent – so the best thing is to wait for confirmation.

  • Rejecting back in the 100.30 resistance should see continuation to the downside.
  • Breaking and closing on the month above 100.60 could lead to a larger USD breakout.

Markets are repricing an imminent FX intervention for the JPY, prompting its outperformance today. Apart from it, the US dollar is flawless in its daily rally.

Month-end flows (throughout the session tomorrow) will be a preview of what's to come!

Safe trades, and keep track of the conflict's progress throughout this week!

data.title: Dallas Fed Manufacturing: Mixed Perceptions In March
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Dallas Fed released its Texas Manufacturing Outlook Survey (TMOS) for March. The general business activity index fell 0.4 points to -0.2, indicating no change in business conditions.

Texas factory activity continued to rise in March, but at a slower pace than the previous month, according to business executives responding to the Texas Manufacturing Outlook Survey. The production index, a key measure of state manufacturing conditions, fell six points to 6.8, a reading suggestive of a below-average pace of output expansion.

Other measures of manufacturing activity also showed signs of slower growth this month. The capacity utilization index moved down five points to 7.2, the new orders index fell to 6.1 from 11.1, and the shipments index fell eight points to 1.8.

Perceptions of broader business conditions were mixed in March. The general business activity index was relatively unchanged at -0.2, with the near-zero reading indicating no change in business activity from February. However, the company outlook index fell into negative territory dropping nearly seven points to -3.5. The outlook uncertainty index shot up 20 points to 26.0, its highest reading since April 2025.

Employment growth stalled and workweeks were flat in March. The employment index came in near zero, with 15 percent of firms noting net hiring and 16 percent of firms noting net layoffs. The hours worked index moved down to 0.9 from 6.1.

Price pressures were little changed while wages grew at a slower pace than last month. Both the finished goods prices index and the raw materials prices index were relatively unchanged at 18.4 and 32.7, respectively. The wages and benefits index fell to 25.2 from 31.9.

Press release

Background on the Texas Manufacturing Outlook Survey (TMOS)

Monthly data for this indicator only dates back to 2004, so it is difficult to see the full potential of this indicator without several business cycles of data. Nevertheless, it is an interesting and important regional manufacturing indicator.

The TMOS is a monthly survey of 100 Texas manufacturers that provides an assessment on the state's factory activity. The survey asks firms whether output, employment, orders, prices, and other indicators have increased, decreased, or remained unchanged over the previous month. Results are aggregated into balance indexes where negative readings indicate contractions while positive ones indicate expansion.

The Dallas Fed on the importance of Texas Manufacturing:

Texas is important to the nation’s manufacturing output. The state produced $296 billion in manufactured goods in 2023, roughly 11 percent of the country’s manufacturing output. Texas ranks second behind California in factory production and first as an exporter of manufactured goods.

Texas turns out a large share of the country’s production of petroleum and coal products, reflecting the significance of the region’s refining industry. Texas also produces over 13 percent of the nation’s chemical products and over 10 percent of nonmetallic mineral products, such as brick, glass and cement.

Here is a snapshot of the complete TMOS.

Texas Manufacturing Survey Future Outlook

The next chart is an overlay of the general business activity index and the future outlook index — the outlook six months ahead. Future general business activity expectations fell to 10.6 this month.

Expectations are for increased manufacturing activity six months from now. The future production index held steady at 35.7, and the future general business activity index remained in positive territory but fell two points to 10.6. Other indexes of future manufacturing activity remained in positive territory and increased.

For comparison, here is the latest ISM Manufacturing survey.

Let's compare all five regional manufacturing indicators. Here is a three-month moving average overlay of each since 2004 (for those with data).

Here is the same chart including the average of the five.

data.title: Carlyle Secured Lending: Dividend Reset Priced In
data.description:
Like so many other private credit platforms, Carlyle Secured Lending Inc. (CGBD) has suffered a significant down-rating of its share price and a compression of its valuation factor amid surging concerns about SaaS loans and capital outflows from the business development company, or BDC, sector. This change in investor sentiment and the resulting repricing of private credit companies has made all BDCs more attractive from a price-to-value point of view, in my opinion.

Carlyle Secured Lending is currently priced at a P/NAV ratio of 0.66X, revealing a potential mispricing that investors can exploit to their benefit. While Carlyle Secured Lending faces a risk of another dividend reset in 2026 amid challenges to NII coverage, I believe a speculative Buy position could be justified nonetheless, as the BDC's valuation implies a high safety margin.

First Lien-Focused BDC Play With Limited SaaS Exposure

Carlyle Secured Lending is heavily oriented towards first liens... which represented approximately 84% of the private credit company’s investment portfolio as of the end of Q4 '25. In addition, the private credit pool made a small number of investments in second liens, equity investments, and investment funds that made up a combined 16% of portfolio assets.

Carlyle Secured Lending has been able to grow its portfolio by 37% in the last year to $2.5B, mainly due to robust demand for new debt capital as well as the 2024 merger with Carlyle Secured Lending III (CSL III). Most importantly, however, the BDC has suffered an increase in non-performing loans in its core middle market portfolio: the non-accrual ratio, based off of fair value, increased a massive 100% year-over-year to 1.2% in the fourth quarter. The non-accrual percentage is therefore about on the same level as the non-accrual ratios of Ares Capital (ARCC), 1.2%, and Blue Owl Capital (OBDC), 1.1%.

Non-Accruals (at cost) 1.0% 2.2% 3.0% 1.6% 1.8% 80.0%

Source: Author.

Carlyle Secured Lending is expanding its portfolio through the acquisition of other private credit vehicles, like CSL III, as well as organic loan origination. In the first instance, Carlyle Secured Lending leverages its large origination platform to add new senior secured loans to its loan pool, allowing the firm to expand its reach and diversify risk. This growth has supported the firm’s expansion in total investment income in the fourth quarter: CGBD's total net investment income grew to $66.9M as of the end of Q4 ’25, showing 19% year-over-year.

A Dividend Reset Is Not Out Of The Question

While the private credit platform has managed to grow its total investment income on the back of the CSL III merger and record originations of $404.7M in the fourth quarter, the BDC has seen net investment income that is about flat on a dollar basis: the firm's net investment income reached $24.0M in the fourth quarter, showing a small decline of 1% compared to the year-earlier period. However, Carlyle Secured Lending issued a large amount of shares in the last year -- its total share count increased 43% year-over-year to $72.6M -- which is in large part due to the merger deal mentioned above.

On a per-share basis, Carlyle Secured Lending's net investment income actually decreased: its net investment income is down approximately 30% Y/Y in the fourth quarter to $0.33 per share, which calculates to a dividend coverage ratio of 82.5%. The dividend coverage trend has worsened in the last year, which is a reflection of the increase in non-performing loans. A dividend cut at this point seems likely, given the coverage trend, but I believe that the market already prices in a dividend reset.

Q4'24 Q1'25 Q2'25 Q3'25 Q4'25 Y/Y Growth
NII $0.47 $0.40 $0.39 $0.37 $0.33 -29.8%
Dividend $0.45 $0.45 $0.40 $0.40 $0.40 -11.1%
Coverage Trend 104.4% 88.9% 97.5% 92.5% 82.5% -21.0%

Source: Author.

Carlyle Secured Lending's Valuation

Like so many BDCs in the marketplace right now, Carlyle Secured Lending is trading at a material discount to net asset value of 34%, which is in part driven by the firm's decline in the dividend coverage ratio, but also by sector-wide fears about the performance of SaaS loans that have been extended to the software sector in recent years. Most importantly, according to Carlyle Secured Lending’s most recent portfolio breakdown from the fourth quarter, software only represented approximately 11% of the BDC's investment assets... so the exposure to the software category is rather limited. Other BDCs, like Ares Capital, have 20% or more exposure to SaaS/software companies.

Carlyle Secured Lending is obviously not the only BDC that is trading at a large discount to net asset value right now. Even top-rated BDCs like Ares Capital are now available for much less than their books show. BDCs with stronger performance records, such as Trinity Capital (TRIN) or Hercules Capital (HTGC), have seen a shrinking of their NAV premium in 2026 but still offer investors plenty of value. Trinity Capital is a buy for me because of its strong origination expansion, while Hercules Capital is set to benefit if SaaS fears turn out to have been exaggerated: 3 Reasons Why The Market Is Wrong.

Carlyle Secured Lending is currently priced ~26% below its 3-year average P/NAV ratio of 0.9X, which is due to the recent fears about SaaS loans and a change in investor sentiment following the outbreak of the Iran war in the Middle East. I believe the market currently prices in a 25%, and I expect the BDC to cut back its dividend to a more sustainable level of $0.30 per share. At this point CGBD would have a dividend coverage ratio, based off of Q4 '25 NII, of 1.1X.

While a dividend cut may loom for Carlyle Secured Lending given a significant NII per-share contraction, the market is overall pessimistic right now with respect to private credit, and the BDC market is broadly oversold. As a result, I see a contrarian revaluation opportunity for CGBD if SaaS concerns subside and the BDC actually removes uncertainty and gets its dividend reset out of the way. I see a fair value for Carlyle Secured Lending of $14.63 per share -- which is derived by applying a 0.9X NAV multiplier to the most up-to-date NAV of $16.26 per share.

Risk With Carlyle Secured Lending

The biggest risk with Carlyle Secured Lending in the short term is a reset of the dividend, which may further darken investor sentiment with respect to this specific BDC. Dividend cuts from other BDCs may also weigh on investor sentiment going forward. What would motivate me to change my mind about Carlyle Secured Lending is if the private credit platform were to institute a larger than 25% dividend cut or if its non-accrual percentage continued to rise.

Final Thoughts

Carlyle Secured Lending is most likely headed for a dividend reset in the short term, in my opinion, given that the private credit company has seen such a serious decline in its net investment income per-share trend. At the same time, other metrics for the BDC, such as its non-accrual percentage, are negative as well. Offsetting this weakness is the fact that the firm has limited SaaS exposure and its shares are already pricing in a dividend cut, and likely much more than that. For contrarian investors with an appetite for risk, a purchase of CGBD could make sense from a risk/reward perspective as well, since shares are priced at depressed levels, reflecting a 34% discount from NAV.

data.title: PSK: Ultra-Long Duration Debt Component At Risk From Debt Cost Revaluations
data.description:
The State Street SPDR ICE Preferred Securities ETF (PSK) is a preferred securities ETF with primarily financial sector exposures at 67% of the portfolio.

Comparing to the iShares US Financials ETF (IYF), which represents a set of the equity stratum of financial exposures as opposed to just preferreds, preferreds and PSK have been more resilient in the face of this year's declines. We are a bit skeptical of the further resilience of the preferreds given some of the trends we are seeing in the yield curve. While there is an equity component to preferreds, there is the unignorable debt component as well—a very high duration debt component. In the face of re-inflation risks, duration bets are a bit of a concern. The equity component is also a concern with higher debt costs incoming and general economic pressure, which affects financial exposures. Therefore, we have concerns that the resilience of the preferreds might not hold.

PSK Breakdown

Preferred instruments tend to have a little higher expense ratio at 0.45% due to the more limited scope of the asset class. PSK is at 0.45%, and this is where comparable preferred security ETFs lie as well, with the (PFF) also at 0.45%. Financial institutions, which are more motivated to financial optimization since it determines risk-weighted assets, the amounts of reserves they have and need, and also the efficiency of their funding source costs with respect to their lending yields, make use of preferred securities more therefore those sectors dominate the exposures.

Necessarily, preferreds have an equity exposure and a debt exposure.

Bear Case

The bear case for the debt exposure is that duration is unsafe in the current environment where markets are re-evaluating their cost of capital expectations. We still don't have consumer inflation expectations logged yet, and the yield curve is already seeing significant upward shifts. The logic of the upward shifts is that both long- and short-term rate expectations are changing. In the short term it's clearly the oil price upside to inflation, the rule of thumb being $10 in oil price per barrel contributes 0.4% to CPI. The war may escalate further with more US troops being posted to the region and with strikes continuing with no obvious abatement. It's unclear whether meaningful ceasefire talks are taking place. The short term also has implications for the longer-term rates, in addition to general conflict and geopolitical fracture premia likely being placed on longer-term structural rates as well due to the inflationary impact of such things. With all rates across the yield curve going up, very long-duration bets, among them preferreds, all have price downside through the debt component.

For the equity component, demand destruction from higher oil prices and the higher debt costs as determined by the market, along with upward pressure on credit spreads, all reflect pressure that could impact the bulk of the exposures in PSK, namely financials. These demand-side risks also spill over into debt and duration risks through credit spreads, which put upward pressure on YTMs and endanger duration. While higher than they have been lately, they are still not at Ukraine war levels, which may be an eventual level that the current conflict could bring, particularly with the consumer weaker than it was in 2022. So maybe there is credit spread upside at the BBB rating level around which the PSK's exposures are concentrated in terms of credit rating. Financials are in general exposed on the equity side to any macro concerns therefore we see downside in the equity component.

Bull Case

The bull case is that maybe ships will start passing through the Strait unharassed by Iran. This may be due to diplomatic successes or Iran's own geopolitical designs. If the hostilities end, that would also achieve the same end. While it may take some time for Gulf capacity to be recovered, there is also hope of more oil production coming from other OPEC+ members who were in the process of unwinding supply cuts and would have excess capacity. This would contribute favorably both to the cost of capital outlook to help the debt component of preferreds and the PSK as well as the equity component through eliminating the demand-side risk of unavoidably higher prices, for example, at the pump, for US consumers.

But we don't see this as particularly likely at the moment and are concerned about troop buildup, among other signs that the conflict cannot be easily resolved at this point.

Bottom Line

The key thing is that the yield curve is shifting upwards across the board, including in the areas that would affect ultra-long duration bets like preferreds, which have no fixed maturity. In addition to that, there is no concurrent help being provided to the equity exposures either. Therefore, the relative resilience of the PSK, particularly with duration meaning sensitivity to long-term rate assumptions by markets, may not be sustained from this point. We'd be more comfortable with instruments that have at least fallen already and negatively responded to the energy crisis. On that basis we'd simply not hazard money here, particularly when cash appears to be favorable in an environment where things could get worse before they get better.

data.title: Exxon Mobil: Boosted By Powerful Pricing And Volume Tailwinds
data.description:
Elevator Pitch

I think Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) is seeing some powerful growth catalysts:

  1. Upstream O&G is the major driver of Exxon Mobil's earnings.
  2. Escalating Iran war tensions provide a favorable backdrop for oil prices.
  3. Production volumes can grow rapidly from low-cost upstream assets.
  4. XOM stock is valued richly vs. peers.
  5. Don't fight the clear and powerful uptrend.

Upstream O&G Is the Major Driver of Exxon Mobil's Earnings

Exxon Mobil operates in 4 key segments:

  1. Upstream: Exploration, production, and sale of oil, natural gas, and LNG.
  2. Energy Products: Refining crude oil derivatives and selling fuels such as gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel.
  3. Chemical Products: Using hydrocarbons to make and sell petrochemicals such as plastics.
  4. Specialty Products: Similar to chemical products but making more specialized materials such as industrial lubricants and greases.

My thesis is focused on the upstream segment. I want to highlight that this is the most important piece for Exxon Mobil's earnings, as it makes up on average around 65% of GAAP segmental earnings:

Escalating Iran War Tensions Provide a Favorable Backdrop for Oil Prices

The Iran war is escalating as the U.S. is getting closer to deploying troops on the ground to potentially safeguard the Strait of Hormuz (a key blockaded chokepoint through which 20% of global oil supply passes), seize Kharg Island (a key Iranian military site and export terminal), or conduct operations against Iran's uranium site in Isfahan to reduce the country's nuclear threat. Already, 3,500 U.S. troops have arrived in the Middle East, and Iranian Parliamentary Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf's rhetoric is... aggressive, to put it mildly.

For the global oil markets, the Strait of Hormuz blockade is the most critical element. Here's some perspective on the scale of the supply shortage this is creating:

A simple back-of-the-envelope calculation suggests the closure of the strait is reducing global oil flows by some 11 million barrels a day, after accounting for the interventions so far aimed at offsetting the loss. When compared with pre-war demand levels, that leaves a roughly 9 million-barrel shortfall — a yawning gap that is more than the oil consumption of the UK, France, Germany, Spain and Italy combined.

- Bloomberg News (emphasis added).

Another clear sign of escalation and potential further supply constraints to the global oil market is the fact that Iran-backed Houthi groups in Yemen are joining the conflict, firing missiles at Israel and threatening to choke off the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, which is another key global shipping route that sees 10-12% of global maritime trade pass through it:

As a result of these escalations, I expect both WTI Crude Oil (CL1:COM) and Brent Oil (CO1:COM) to continue increasing and remain above $100/bbl at a minimum:

According to Bloomberg, the U.S. government and Wall Street are starting to more seriously consider the prospect of $200/bbl oil prices. Obviously, this is a major windfall tailwind for upstream O&G players like Exxon Mobil. My analysis shows that its oil commodity realization prices have virtually perfect correlation to Crude and Brent prices:

Production Volumes Can Grow Rapidly From Low-Cost Upstream Assets

Exxon Mobil is increasing upstream production at a rapid clip of almost 10% YoY on a 4-quarter average basis:

In recent quarters, the company tended to rather consistently deliver modest production surprises vs. consensus for total average daily production in millions of barrels of oil equivalents (MBOE):

The company's upstream production sites in the Permian Basin and Guyana made up over half of the company's total upstream production in 2025. And these 2 sites are expected to increase production volumes at a rapid clip over the next 5 years. For example, Exxon's production at the Permian Basin is expected to grow at 9.3% CAGR:

For Guyana, the company is on track to increase production capacity to 1.7M BOE/d by 2030. Given this, I'm assuming a production cadence of 1.6, as I believe production utilization will be high, but it's a bit aggressive to assume 100%. This still leads to a very rapid 18.0% CAGR growth estimate over the next 5 years:

The best part is that these 2 sites have low production costs. For example, the breakeven oil price level for the Guyana site is already at $30/bbl. And the company is targeting a global breakeven oil cost of $30/bbl by 2030, enabled a lot by more than 40 technological innovations to improve operational efficiency in the Permian Basin operations.

For context on how low these breakeven costs are, here's what the surveyed breakeven oil cost level was among 92 US upstream O&G firms and locations:

The bottom line is that there are plenty of volume tailwinds ahead as well.

XOM Stock is Valued Richly vs. Peers

XOM's 1-yr fwd EV/EBITDA is at 10.0x, which corresponds to a 49% premium vs. its comps' median multiple of 6.7x:

This chart below shows that XOM is trading at a much higher premium vs. its comps than the typical 27%, which is an argument for relatively stretched valuations:

Don't Fight the Clear and Powerful Uptrend

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return, as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

XOM Technical Analysis

This is very simple. The XOM monthly chart shows a very clear, powerful uptrend. And there is no reason to fight the trend, as there are zero signs of sellers or bearish pressure.

Takeaway and Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Bullish due to compelling pricing and volume catalysts.
  • Valuation: Bearish due to rich valuation vs. peers.
  • Technical Analysis: Bullish due to impressive bullish momentum with no signs of sellers.
  • Final XOM Assessment and Rating: Bullish, "Buy."
